original_abstract:
Ultrafine tuning of metal-organic framework (MOF) architectures at sub-angstrom or picometer scales is a powerful way to enhance separation selectivity for gases with nearly identical molecular dimensions. However, in MOFs with relatively large pores, the impact of such subtle modifications on adsorption behavior is often negligible. In this work, we present an integrative design strategy that synergizes extreme pore compression with ultrafine structural tuning. This approach exploits the partitioned *acs* (*pacs*) platform, which possesses a unique multimodular framework and an exceptional tolerance for isoreticular substitution.

By utilizing a primary ligand module (L1), we first compressed the *pacs* pore size to its practical limit; specifically, replacing prototypical 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate with *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate resulted in a structural compression ratio of approximately 30% (measured by the unit cell *c/a* ratio). We then employed a secondary module (L2) to perform ultrafine tuning, adjusting the pore environment by less than 3%. This dual-ligand strategy dramatically enhanced C₂H₂/CO₂ selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8, leading to superior performance in experimental gas breakthrough studies. As the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate capable of mimicking para-substituted benzene moieties through a bioisosteric (BIS) approach, *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate opens new avenues for the precision engineering of MOF-based separations.
humanized_abstract:
Ultrafine tuning of metal-organic framework (MOF) structures at sub-angstrom or picometer levels is an efficient method to achieve desirable separation selectivity for slightly different-sized gases with similar dimensions. Unfortunately, the effects of ultrafine-tuning on gas adsorption in large-pore MOFs are almost negligible. Herein, an integrated design strategy has been established by balancing extreme pore compression with ultrafine structural tuning. A multimodular structure with highly isoreticular exchangeability was achieved on the basis of the partitioned acyclic substituted (acs, *acs*) or polyacyclic substituted (pacs, *pacs*) platform, which exhibits flexible framework geometry and a high adaptability toward isoreticular substitutions and extensions, thereby providing unique structural merits for developing highly efficient MOF materials with tailored adsorption properties for different guest molecules.

Through utilization of a primary ligand module named L1, the *pacs* channel size is maximally minimized, via replacement of the 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate (1,4-BDC) noda with *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate with a 30% structural compression as defined by a unit cell *c/a* ratio. Furthermore, an optimized secondary ligand module named L2, enables minimal adjustments on the less-than-3% pore-channel space environment. Through the employment of both L1 and L2 modules, the CO2/C2H2 selectivity is increased from 2.6 up to 20.8 as confirmed in individual breakthrough experiments. *trans*-1,3-CBD as the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate noda within MOFs serves not only as a functional *BIS* with para-substituted benzene of adsorbable molecules but with great opportunity in future synthesis of other MOFs suited to high-performance adsorption-based separation.
